questions.promptTex: \text{If } AB \text{ is a diameter of a circle and } P \text{ is on the circle (not at } A \text{ or } B), \\ \text{what is } \angle APB \text{?}
hints: Always 90° regardless of where P is.

questions.promptTex: \text{A circle has diameter } AB = 26. \, P \text{ is on the circle with } AP = 10. \\ \text{Find } BP.
hints: Pythagoras: $AP^2 + BP^2 = AB^2$, so $100 + BP^2 = 676$. (Recognize 10-24-26 = 2× the 5-12-13 triple.)
questions.explanation: By Thales, $\angle APB = 90°$. Pythagoras: $BP = \sqrt{26^2 - 10^2} = \sqrt{676 - 100} = \sqrt{576} = 24$. (10-24-26 is a Pythagorean triple.)

steps.text: **Thales' Theorem:** since $AB$ is a diameter, $\angle APB = 90°$. So $\triangle APB$ is right-angled at $P$.

questions.prompt: The converse of Thales' Theorem says:
choices: If $\angle APB = 90°$, then $P$ lies on a circle with $AB$ as diameter.
If $\angle APB = 90°$, then $AB$ is a side of a square.
If $\angle APB = 90°$, then $P = A$ or $P = B$.
If $P$ is on a circle, then $\angle APB = 90°$ for all chords $AB$.
hints: Right angle viewing of $AB$ ⇒ $P$ sits on the Thales circle of $AB$.
questions.explanation: The converse: the locus of points $P$ with $\angle APB = 90°$ is the circle with $AB$ as diameter (the Thales circle).

questions.promptTex: \text{A right triangle has its hypotenuse as the diameter of a circumscribed circle.} \\ \text{If the hypotenuse is 16, what is the radius of the circle?}
questions.explanation: By Thales (and its converse), the hypotenuse of a right triangle is the diameter of its circumscribed circle. Radius = $16/2 = 8$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Point } P \text{ is on a semicircle with diameter } AB = 20. \\ \text{If } AP = 12, \text{ find the area of } \triangle APB.
hints: Thales: right angle at $P$, so $AP$ and $BP$ are the legs.
questions.explanation: Right triangle with legs 12 and 16 (since $\sqrt{400 - 144} = 16$). Area $= \tfrac{1}{2}(12)(16) = 96$.

steps.text: **By Thales,** $\angle APB = 90°$. So $AP$ and $BP$ are the legs of a right triangle with hypotenuse $AB$.

questions.prompt: In which of these triangles is the circumcenter located on a side of the triangle?
choices: Right triangles (on the hypotenuse, specifically at its midpoint)
hints: Thales tells us the right angle vertex sees the hypotenuse as a diameter.
So the hypotenuse is the diameter of the circumcircle.
questions.explanation: By the converse of Thales: a right triangle's hypotenuse is the diameter of its circumscribed circle. So the circumcenter is the midpoint of the hypotenuse — on the triangle itself. Acute triangles have circumcenter inside; obtuse have it outside.

questions.promptTex: \text{A right triangle is inscribed in a circle of radius } r = 6.5. \\ \text{If one leg is 5, find the other leg.}

questions.prompt: Three points A, B, C lie on a circle. ∠ACB = 90°. What is true about segment AB?
choices: AB is a diameter of the circle.
AB is perpendicular to the radius at C.
hints: An inscribed angle of 90° ⇒ subtended chord is a diameter.
questions.explanation: By the converse of Thales, if an inscribed angle is 90°, the chord it subtends is a diameter.
